expected_output:
sequenceDiagram
participant User
participant MobileApp
participant BackendForFrontend
participant PaymentGateway
participant Database

Note over User: Online payment processing
User->>MobileApp: Select products and proceed to checkout
MobileApp->>BackendForFrontend: Payment request (amount, payment method)
activate BackendForFrontend
BackendForFrontend->>PaymentGateway: Forward payment details
activate PaymentGateway
alt payment fails
  PaymentGateway-->>BackendForFrontend: Error code
  BackendForFrontend-->>MobileApp: Error message
else payment is successful
  PaymentGateway-->>BackendForFrontend: Transaction ID
  BackendForFrontend->>Database: Update transaction status
  activate Database
  Database-->>BackendForFrontend: Transaction updated
  deactivate Database
  BackendForFrontend-->>MobileApp: Transaction confirmation
  MobileApp->>User: Order confirmation
end

deactivate PaymentGateway
deactivate BackendForFrontend
llm_response:
```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant MA as Mobile App
    participant BFF as BFF
    participant PG as Payment Gateway
    participant DB as Database

    Note over U,MA: User selects products & proceeds to checkout
    MA->>BFF: POST /payment(details)
    activate BFF

    alt Payment Success
        BFF->>PG: POST /processPayment(details)
        activate PG
        PG-->>BFF: 200 OK (transactionId)
        deactivate PG

        BFF->>DB: UPDATE payment SET status='Authorized' WHERE id=transactionId
        activate DB
        DB-->>BFF: OK
        deactivate DB

        BFF-->>MA: 200 OK (confirmation, transactionId)
        deactivate BFF

        MA->>U: Show order confirmation
    else Payment Failure
        BFF->>PG: POST /processPayment(details)
        activate PG
        PG-->>BFF: Error (code, message)
        deactivate PG

        BFF-->>MA: Error (code, message)
        deactivate BFF

        MA->>U: Show payment error message
    end
```
llm_judge_syntax_response: 0.950; The diagram uses valid MermaidJS sequenceDiagram syntax: all participants are declared with `participant` (with aliases, which is allowed), messages use correct `->>` / `-->>` notation, and the `alt` / `else` / `end` block is properly formed and closed. Activations and deactivations are mostly balanced: `BFF`, `PG`, and `DB` are each activated and later deactivated within their respective branches. The only minor structural issue is that `BFF` is activated once before the `alt` and then deactivated separately in each branch; this is logically acceptable but can be seen as slightly asymmetric, since the activation spans the conditional while deactivation is branch-specific. Overall, it should render correctly in Mermaid.
llm_judge_mermaid_only_response: 1.000; The response is fully wrapped in a Markdown code block with ```mermaid, contains only valid Mermaid sequenceDiagram syntax, has no extra narration or formatting outside the code block, and appears clean, parsable, and renderable by Mermaid.
llm_judge_logic_response: 0.950; Syntax is valid MermaidJS, participants are ordered correctly, and success/failure alternate flows are clearly modeled with proper activations/deactivations inside each branch. Every external request (Mobile App→BFF, BFF→PG, BFF→DB) has a corresponding response. The main interactions from the prompt (selection/checkout, payment request, gateway processing, authorization/failure, DB update, confirmation/error to app, and user notification) are all covered, with only minor deviations from the reference (e.g., using a note instead of an explicit User→App selection message, and deactivating BFF inside each branch rather than once after `end`). No nested decisions were required, so that criterion is trivially satisfied. Overall, the logic and flow completeness closely match the expected solution.
llm_judge_completeness_response: 0.950; Syntax is valid MermaidJS and participants are correctly ordered and complete (User, Mobile App, BFF, Payment Gateway, Database). The main flows are all present: user checkout (noted), mobile app sending payment to BFF, BFF forwarding to payment gateway, gateway responding with either success (transactionId) or error, BFF updating the database on success, and the mobile app showing either confirmation or error to the user. Each request has a corresponding response, and success vs. failure are modeled with an `alt` block, satisfying the decision-point and error-path requirements. Minor deviations from the prompt are acceptable (e.g., using a note instead of an explicit User→MobileApp selection message, using SQL-like text instead of REST for the DB call), and activations/deactivations are mostly correct, though BFF is deactivated inside each branch rather than once after the `alt`, which is slightly less clean but not functionally incorrect. Overall coverage and correctness are very high with only minor stylistic/structural issues.
llm_judge_activation_handling_response: 0.950; Syntax is valid MermaidJS. Activations are used consistently when a participant starts processing (BFF, PG, DB) and deactivated after their responses. Every `activate` has a corresponding `deactivate`, and there are no `deactivate` calls without a prior `activate`. The only minor issue is that BFF is deactivated inside each branch of the `alt`, which is correct but slightly redundant; deactivating once after the `alt` block would be a bit cleaner. Overall, the activation/deactivation usage clearly improves readability and traceability.
llm_judge_error_and_status_tracking_resp: 0.900; Syntax is valid MermaidJS and activations/deactivations are mostly correct. Success and failure flows are clearly separated with an `alt Payment Success / else Payment Failure` block, and each request has a corresponding response. Error handling is explicit: the failure branch shows the gateway returning an error (code, message) and the BFF propagating it to the app, which then shows an error to the user. Status updates are present but only on the success path (DB update to `status='Authorized'` and confirmation back to the app); the failure path does not update or log status in the DB, so state tracking is asymmetric. Overall, the diagram handles error cases and status updates clearly and effectively, but could better track entity state on failure (e.g., recording failed payment status), preventing a perfect score.
